title: Stage 1b: Learning Rate Fix
text:
6 What We Learned
Stage 1 → 1b is a textbook case of learning rate as the binding constraint. The architecture, data, and loss function were all adequate — the model failed to converge only because the optimizer overshot the loss minimum on every update. Three theoretical concepts explain the full picture.

6.1 1. Overshoot ≠ Overfitting
A common misreading of Stage 1’s learning curve would be “the model overfit after epoch 6.” This is wrong. Overfitting produces a signature where training loss keeps improving while validation loss degrades — the model memorizes training examples at the expense of generalization. Stage 1 showed the opposite: both train and val AUC degraded together after epoch 6. This is the signature of optimization instability, not statistical overfitting.
The distinction matters because the remedy is different. Overfitting calls for regularization (dropout, weight decay, data augmentation, early stopping). Optimization instability calls for learning rate reduction — which is exactly what fixed it.


6.2 2. The Loss Landscape Geometry
With 6,849 parameters, unet_tiny defines a loss surface in ~7,000-dimensional space. At LR = 5e-5, each Adam update moved the parameter vector by a step large enough to jump across narrow valleys in this surface. The optimizer found the right valley early (epoch 6, AUC = 0.93) but could not settle into it — each subsequent step bounced the parameters to a worse region.
At LR = 5e-6, the step size is 10x smaller. The optimizer navigates the same landscape but can now descend into narrow valleys without overshooting. The monotonic convergence in Figure 1 confirms this: the optimizer followed a smooth downhill path for 60 epochs before reaching the valley floor.
This connects to a general result in optimization theory: for a loss function with Lipschitz-continuous gradients (smoothness constant \(L\)), convergence is guaranteed only when \(\eta < 2/L\). Stage 1’s LR exceeded this bound; Stage 1b’s did not. We did not need to compute \(L\) explicitly — the learning curve itself is the diagnostic.


6.3 3. Adaptive Scheduling as Coarse-to-Fine Optimization
ReduceLROnPlateau implements a simple but effective coarse-to-fine strategy. At the initial LR (5e-6), the optimizer takes relatively large steps — good for covering distance toward the optimum. Once progress stalls (5 epochs without val AUC improvement), the LR halves, enabling finer adjustments. This repeated six times (5e-6 → 2.5e-6 → … → 1e-7), each halving squeezing out a small additional gain.
The LR schedule table above shows the diminishing returns clearly: the six halvings after epoch 33 collectively added only +0.0013 AUC. This is consistent with the optimizer having already reached a flat region of the loss surface — additional precision in the step size yields negligible improvement. The model has converged.


6.4 4. Probability Calibration: Why the Threshold Shift Matters
The most consequential change is not the AUC improvement but the decision threshold shift from 0.15 to 0.90. In Stage 1, the model produced poorly separated probability distributions — building and non-building pixels overlapped heavily, requiring a very low threshold to capture buildings (at the cost of false positives). In Stage 1b, the distributions are well-separated: buildings cluster near \(p = 1.0\) and non-buildings near \(p = 0.0\).
This has a direct practical consequence for Stage 2. When we aggregate predicted building density per district, we need the raw probability map to be interpretable as a confidence-weighted density estimate. A well-calibrated model (threshold ≈ 0.5–0.9) produces probability maps where pixel values approximate the true probability of a building being present. A poorly calibrated model (threshold = 0.15) produces maps where the raw values are not meaningful — you can threshold them but cannot average them. Stage 1b’s calibration makes the probability map directly usable as input to district-level aggregation.


6.5 5. Val > Train AUC: Distribution Shift, Not a Bug
Throughout training, validation AUC exceeds training AUC (Figure 1). This is unusual but not problematic. The spatial train/val split assigns southern/central Rwanda (rows 0–2, including dense Kigali) to training and northern Rwanda (row 3, sparser rural areas) to validation. Building detection in sparse rural areas is an easier discrimination problem — fewer ambiguous pixels, clearer contrast between buildings and surrounding vegetation. The model trained on the harder distribution (dense urban) generalizes well to the easier distribution (sparse rural), which is the favorable direction for our application.
If anything, this increases confidence in the model’s robustness: it was trained on the most difficult region and still performs well on a geographically distinct area.

title: Stage 1b: Learning Rate Fix
section: 7 Possible Improvements vs. Moving to Stage 2
text:
7 Possible Improvements vs. Moving to Stage 2
A natural question: should we keep tuning Stage 1 or move forward? Below we evaluate the most promising hyperparameter improvements and argue that Stage 2 is the correct next step.

7.1 Improvements That Could Help









Technique
Expected Gain
Effort
Worth It?




LR warmup (1e-7 → 5e-6 over 5 epochs)
Small — prevents early instability, but Stage 1b already converges smoothly
Low
Not now — no instability to fix


Cosine annealing (replace ReduceLROnPlateau)
Marginal — smoother decay, but plateau-based already works well
Low
Optional for Stage 1c

0.001–0.005 AUC — find optimal initial LR
Medium (4x training time)

More epochs (200+)
Negligible — curve already flat for 43 epochs at min_lr
Low
No — already converged

Potentially significant — more spectral information
High (GPU needed)
Yes — this is buildingsALL on Quest


Data augmentation (flips, rotations)
Small for binary detection, more useful for regression
Medium
Defer to Stage 3





7.2 Why We Move to Stage 2 Now
Three reasons:

The model has converged. 43 epochs at min_lr with no improvement is strong evidence that unet_tiny has extracted all it can from 3 bands. Further LR tuning will not break through the label ceiling (~0.97–0.98 F1 imposed by Open Buildings accuracy).
The binding constraint has shifted. At AUC = 0.976, the bottleneck is no longer “can the model detect buildings?” — it clearly can. The question is now “does building density correlate with economic activity?” That is a Stage 2 question (district-level validation against census data), not a Stage 1 question.
Architecture improvements run in parallel. The buildingsALL-Rwanda2022 experiment (unet_medium, 10 bands) requires GPU and will run on Quest. This tests whether additional spectral bands (Red Edge, SWIR1, etc.) improve discrimination. But we do not need to wait for it — Stage 2 validation can proceed with the current 3-band model, and if the 10-band model improves, we re-run Stage 2 with the better checkpoint.



7.3 What Would Change This Decision
We would return to Stage 1 tuning if:

Stage 2 reveals that AUC = 0.976 is not sufficient for meaningful district-level correlation (e.g., Pearson r < 0.3). This would suggest the model’s pixel-level accuracy is too low for aggregation to work, and further improvement is needed.
The 10-band Quest model significantly outperforms 3-band (e.g., AUC > 0.99), suggesting that architecture/data was the bottleneck, not optimization. In that case, further hyperparameter tuning of the larger model would be warranted.

Neither outcome blocks us from starting Stage 2 now.

8 Bug Fix: plot_metric_progress() Crash
The training run crashed at the end during post-training plotting:
KeyError: 'precision'
  at vendor/mlgis-ponds/src/mlgis_helpers/evaluation.py:222
Root cause: plot_metric_progress() unconditionally accessed progress["precision"], progress["recall"], and progress["f1"] — but the _save_final_results() function in training.py only populates progress["auc"] and progress["train_auc"] from the Keras training history. Keras does not compute per-epoch precision/recall by default.
Fix applied: Guarded lines 222–224 with if "key" in progress: checks. The function now plots whichever metrics are available and skips the rest. This crash prevented val_metrics.json and training progress plots from being saved, but the best.keras model checkpoint (saved during training via ModelCheckpoint callback) was unaffected.

title: Stage 1: Building Detection, Rwanda
text:
The goal of this project is to produce a pixel-level (10 m) economic density map for Africa and eventually the world, using free Sentinel-2 satellite imagery. The resolution target is 25× to 2,500× finer than the best existing global estimates.


Three papers bracket the space this project aims to fill:









Paper
Geography
Resolution
Method




Khachiyan et al. (2022)
United States
~1.2 km
Daytime imagery → GDP


Rossi-Hansberg et al. (2025)
Global
25 km
Random forest, OECD extrapolation


This project
Africa → Global
10 m
U-Net + Sentinel-2, trained on censuses



Rossi-Hansberg (2025) is the current benchmark for a global microspatial GDP map. This project’s target is the same outcome at 2,500× finer resolution, trained on actual establishment censuses rather than OECD extrapolation.




Rwanda — NISR Establishment Census 2020 + 2023 (232k–269k firms). No GPS coordinates → used for district-level validation only. Google Open Buildings v3 provides pixel-level training labels.
Peru — Fine-resolution sales/GDP data (via Michigan PhD student, through Sebastian Sardon). GPS-coded → will be the primary regression training country. Pending data access.

The plan: train on Rwanda + Peru → apply globally via free Sentinel-2 → global 10m economic density map.



Rwanda’s census records district (province/district) but not GPS location per firm. The workaround is Google Open Buildings v3, a machine-learning derived building footprint dataset covering sub-Saharan Africa at 10m resolution with confidence ≥ 0.75.
Buildings are a strong proxy for economic activity: firms need physical space, and building density at 10m captures the spatial concentration of economic activity far better than nighttime lights (which saturate in cities and are zero in rural areas). Stage 2 (below) will validate this claim empirically at the district level.

text:
2 Data

2.1 Sentinel-2 Imagery

Source: Google Earth Engine — COPERNICUS/S2_SR_HARMONIZED
Coverage: All of Rwanda (~26,000 km²)
Year: 2022 annual median composite (bridges 2020 and 2023 census vintages)
Cloud masking: SCL band + s2cloudless probability < 20%
Bands: 10 Sentinel-2 bands (Blue, Green, Red, Red Edge 1–3, NIR, NIR2, SWIR1, SWIR2) + 2 derived indices (NDVI, MNDWI)
Resolution: 10 m native; resampled to 10 m grid
Storage: 20 tiles of 0.5° × 0.5° each (~500 MB/tile), ~10 GB processed

The imagery was downloaded via GEE batch export to Google Drive, then transferred locally with rclone. No cloud costs incurred (GEE free tier).


2.2 Building Labels

Source: Google Open Buildings v3 — GOOGLE/Research/open-buildings/v3/polygons
Confidence threshold: ≥ 0.75
Format: Binary raster, 10m, EPSG:4326 — value 1 = building footprint pixel
Known quality: ~85% recall in urban Rwanda, ~60% in rural areas (Sirko et al. 2021)

The map below shows building pixel density per 0.5° training tile. The spatial heterogeneity is large: Kigali (col 2, row 1) has 694k building pixels vs. 12k in the sparse south-west corner — a 58× range. The model must generalize across this variation.






Figure 1: Building pixel counts per 0.5° tile. Blue borders = validation set (held out during training). Numbers show building pixels × 10³.





2.3 Census Data (for Stage 2 validation)

title: Stage 1: Building Detection, Rwanda
text:
3 Stage 1: Binary Building Detection

3.1 What Was Trained
A U-Net (encoder–decoder convolutional neural network) was trained to perform binary pixel classification: given a 128×128 patch of Sentinel-2 imagery, predict which 10m pixels contain building footprints.
Architecture: unet_tiny — 6,849 parameters, 2-level encoder, 4 base filters. Deliberately small: the goal at this stage is to verify that the spectral signal is recoverable from Sentinel-2, not to achieve maximum capacity.
Input bands: NIR (B08), Blue (B02), SWIR2 (B12) — the three bands most discriminative for built-up surfaces. NIR > Green at built-up is a well-established spectral rule.
Loss function: Focal Tversky loss with α=0.7, β=0.3, γ=4/3. This upweights false negatives (missed buildings) relative to false positives — appropriate for spatially sparse targets (only ~1.4% of all pixels are buildings).


3.2 Train/Validation Split
The split is spatial (not random), which is the correct choice for geospatial models. A random split would allow patches from the same geographic area in both train and val, causing optimistic estimates of generalization.
Rwanda chunk grid (0.5° tiles, col = west→east, row = south→north):

All metrics computed via sliding-window inference over the full validation set (2 tiles, ~75k pixels).



4.1 Interpreting the Metrics
AUC = 0.93 is the area under the ROC curve. Concretely: if we pick one random building pixel and one random non-building pixel, the model ranks the building pixel higher 93% of the time. This is a threshold-free measure of discriminative ability.
Recall = 0.998 at threshold 0.15 means the model detects essentially all labeled building pixels. The low threshold (0.15 rather than the conventional 0.50) reflects the deliberate bias toward sensitivity: for economic density mapping, systematically missing buildings is worse than occasionally flagging non-buildings, because we aggregate predictions across many pixels per district.
F1 = 0.97 is close to the realistic ceiling given the training labels. Open Buildings v3 has a known miss rate of ~15% in urban Rwanda and ~40% in rural Rwanda (Sirko et al. 2021). A model that correctly detects a real building not present in Open Buildings is penalized as a false positive. The effective quality ceiling on F1 against this label source is approximately 0.97–0.98.


4.2 Learning Curve









Figure 2: Training and validation metrics over epochs











Figure 3: ROC curve — final model on validation set












Figure 4: Early stopping trace — patience counter vs validation AUC









Figure 5: Train AUC vs Val AUC over all 26 epochs. The gap after epoch 6 is distribution shift (Kigali-heavy train vs northern Rwanda val), not capacity overfitting — unet_tiny has only 6,849 parameters.



The model learned quickly (AUC = 0.89 after epoch 1, 0.93 by epoch 6) and then degraded. The diagnostic: both training and validation AUC decline together after epoch 6. This rules out standard overfitting (which would show train improving while val deteriorates). The correct diagnosis is learning rate overshoot: at α = 5×10⁻⁵, the optimizer takes steps large enough to cross the loss minimum and land on the other side on each iteration. The model found a good solution at epoch 6 but the optimizer couldn’t converge around it. See the Learning Dynamics section below for the full analysis and the Stage 1b fix. Early stopping correctly captures the epoch-6 snapshot as the best model; the degradation afterward is an optimization artifact, not a model failure.


4.3 What the Model Sees — Example Patch (Kigali)
The model ingests 128×128 patches (1.28 km × 1.28 km at 10m resolution) in three bands: NIR, Blue, and SWIR2. Below is a representative patch from the densest training tile (chunk_2_1, Kigali area).






Figure 6: Left to right: NIR band (vegetation bright, built-up dark), SWIR2 band (built-up bright), false-color composite (NIR→R, Blue→G, SWIR2→B — built-up areas appear magenta/purple), Open Buildings v3 label (red = building pixel). The spectral contrast between built-up and non-built-up is strong in NIR and SWIR2 — the primary signal the model learns.

The question Stage 2 answers: does predicted building density actually track economic activity?

r

Proxy too weak → revisit label source or model



The 30-district correlation is a deliberately conservative test: it requires the model’s predictions to be not just pixel-accurate (Stage 1) but spatially aggregated into an economically meaningful signal. Even if the pixel-level F1 were 0.99, a spatially biased model (e.g., one that only detects Kigali) would fail this test.

title: Stage 1: Building Detection, Rwanda
text:
6 What Comes After (Stage 3–4)
Once Stage 2 validates the building-density proxy:
Stage 3 — Regression head: Replace the binary output layer with a continuous GDP density predictor. Train on Peru fine-resolution sales data (GPS-coded, via Michigan PhD student through Sebastian Sardon — currently pending data access). Rwanda provides held-out validation. The regression head predicts log GDP per 10m pixel.
Stage 4 — Global application: Run inference over all of sub-Saharan Africa using free Sentinel-2 composites from GEE. Aggregate to any desired spatial unit (grid cell, district, country).
The pipeline is already structured for this: the U-Net architecture, GEE download scripts, and preprocessing are all country-agnostic. Adding Peru requires only a new config block and relabeling the output head.

title: Stage 1: Building Detection, Rwanda
text:
8 Diagnostics and Planned Improvements

8.1 Precision-Recall Curve (Sebastian’s Feedback)
ROC AUC alone is insufficient for heavily imbalanced classification problems. With only 0.54% of validation pixels being building pixels (rural northern Rwanda is sparser than the training set), a model can achieve high ROC AUC simply by ranking most negatives low — even if precision at high recall collapses. The Precision-Recall curve is the right diagnostic here.






Figure 7: Precision-recall curve from sliding-window inference over both validation chunks (chunk_2_3 and chunk_3_3). The dashed baseline shows what a random classifier would achieve at the 0.54% positive rate. The red dot is our operating point at threshold=0.15.



At our operating point (threshold = 0.15): - Precision = 0.951 — 95% of flagged pixels are real buildings - Recall = 0.998 — we detect essentially every labeled building pixel
The curve shape confirms the model is genuinely discriminative, not just exploiting the class imbalance. Average Precision (area under the PR curve) is plotted directly on the figure.

Why threshold = 0.15 rather than 0.50? The model output is a probability map that is later aggregated spatially (mean building density per district). At the aggregation stage, missing a building (false negative) is worse than a small false positive, because the economic density signal comes from averaging many pixels. The low threshold deliberately prioritizes recall.




8.2 Recommended Additions: mIoU and PIoU (Sebastian’s Feedback)
Sebastian flagged two segmentation metrics not yet computed:
mIoU — mean Intersection-over-Union, the standard summary metric for semantic segmentation benchmarks. For each class, IoU = TP / (TP + FP + FN); mIoU = mean over building and background. Unlike AUC (ranking) or F1 (threshold-dependent), mIoU directly penalizes spatial imprecision in the predicted mask boundary.
PIoU ≥ 0.95 — fraction of ground truth instances where the predicted mask achieves ≥95% geometric overlap. This is an instance-level metric used by the Fields of the World benchmark (Kerner et al. 2024), a multi-country Sentinel-2 field delineation dataset that is the closest public benchmark to this project’s setting (same imagery, same pixel-level segmentation approach). A PIoU ≥ 0.95 target is stringent — requiring near-exact boundary delineation, not just approximate localization.
Both metrics are planned additions to Stage 1b evaluation. For the building-density proxy goal (aggregating predictions to the district level), mIoU is the most relevant: a spatially imprecise mask that over-smooths building boundaries would systematically undercount buildings after spatial aggregation.



8.3 Learning Dynamics Diagnosis
Sebastian identified a key issue from the AUC learning curve: the model peaks at epoch 6 (val AUC = 0.930) and then degrades monotonically to 0.623 by epoch 26 — and crucially, train AUC also decreases (0.906 → 0.827). This is not standard overfitting (which would show train increasing while val decreases). Instead, both curves move together downward — the hallmark of learning rate instability.
Bayesian interpretation: Each epoch is a posterior update. With a learning rate of 5×10⁻⁵, the parameter updates at each step are large enough that after the model finds a good region of parameter space (epoch 6), the optimizer overshoots that region on subsequent steps and wanders toward worse solutions. The model never converges — it keeps moving.
Proposed fixes (in order of priority):








Fix
Mechanism
Implementation




1. Reduce base LR
Smaller steps → less overshoot
learning_rate: 5e-6 in config (10× reduction)


2. LR schedule: ReduceLROnPlateau
Halve LR when val_auc stalls
Add Keras callback: factor=0.5, patience=5, monitor='val_auc'


3. LR warmup + cosine decay
Ramp up for 3 epochs, anneal from epoch 6
Cosine schedule with warmup (standard for transformer/CNN training)


4. Gradient clipping
Cap large gradient updates directly
Already present (AdamW with clipnorm=1.0) — may need tightening



The current config uses AdamW (Adam with weight decay) with weight_decay=0.05 and clipnorm=1.0. AdamW is the right optimizer. The problem is the learning rate magnitude relative to the loss landscape, not the optimizer choice.
Experiment plan for Stage 1b (on Quest, buildingsALL-Rwanda2022):
# config/config.yaml — ARCHITECTURE_HPARAMS override for next run
unet_medium:
  learning_rate: 5.0e-6   # was 5e-5 — 10× reduction
  base_filters: 16
  depth: 3

title: Stage 1: Building Detection, Rwanda
text:
9 Anticipated Questions
Pre-empting likely PI review questions.
Q: The model peaked at epoch 6 and degraded after — is this a failure? No. Early stopping correctly identified epoch 6 as the best checkpoint. The degradation after epoch 6 is a learning rate problem (α = 5×10⁻⁵ too high → optimizer overshoots), not a model quality failure. Stage 1b, with LR 10× lower and adaptive reduction, is designed to produce monotonic convergence. AUC = 0.93 is the genuine model quality at that point; the issue is purely that the optimizer couldn’t converge around the solution it found.
Q: Your labels come from Google Open Buildings — itself an ML model. Isn’t this circular? There is a known label ceiling: the model can’t systematically exceed the quality of its labels. Open Buildings v3 misses ~15–40% of buildings depending on density (Sirko et al. 2021). We report this ceiling explicitly: F1 ≈ 0.97–0.98 is near the practical maximum against this label source. The economic density signal — aggregated over thousands of pixels per district — is robust to individual-pixel label noise: a 15% random miss rate across many buildings yields an unbiased district-level density estimate. The circularity concern is more important for individual building delineation than for the aggregate proxy use case.
Q: Why buildings as a proxy? Aren’t nighttime lights better/standard? Nighttime lights saturate in cities and are dark even in areas with significant informal daytime activity. Buildings are visible in free daytime imagery, at 10m resolution (2,500× finer), and do not saturate. The proxy assumption — building density correlates with economic activity — is empirically tested in Stage 2 (30-district Pearson r). Stage 2 passes or fails this claim; it is not assumed.
Q: Only 30 districts for Stage 2 — isn’t that a very small sample? Rwanda has 30 administrative districts. At r = 0.7 with n = 30, statistical power is adequate to clear a “>0.7” threshold (p < 0.0001). What n = 30 cannot do is distinguish r = 0.75 from r = 0.80 — which is not required. The threshold design is deliberately binary: proxy valid or not valid. If valid, we proceed; if not, we revise the label source or model.
Q: Open Buildings recall is ~85% urban and ~60% rural. Doesn’t spatial bias corrupt district validation? Yes — this is the key caveat. Urban districts will have higher-quality predictions than rural ones. We plan to test for this explicitly: regress Stage 2 residuals against district urbanization rate. If bias is systematic, apply an urban/rural accuracy correction before the Pearson r comparison. This is documented as a Stage 2 diagnostic step.
Q: Why 3 bands (NIR, Blue, SWIR2) and not all 10? These three capture the built-up spectral signature most efficiently (NIR: dark over built-up; SWIR2: bright over built-up; Blue: separates soil and urban from vegetation). Using all 10 bands is configured separately (buildingsALL-Rwanda2022) and is a one-line config change. The 3-band version validates the spectral signal at minimal model complexity; adding bands is an upgrade once the pipeline is confirmed working.
Q: What’s the realistic ceiling for this project? Will you hit Khachiyan / Rossi-Hansberg journals? The gap is clear: no existing paper delivers a pixel-level (10m) global economic density map trained on actual establishment censuses. Khachiyan (2022) is US-only at 1.2km; Rossi-Hansberg (2025) is global but at 25km using OECD extrapolation. If Stage 2 validates the building proxy and Peru data provides the regression training signal, the contribution is novel at the top-5 level. The bottleneck is Peru fine-GDP data access (via Sebastian → Michigan PhD student).

title: Stage 2: District Validation + Error Analysis
text:
This report documents Stage 2 — the first end-to-end test of whether our building detection model produces an economically meaningful signal at the district level. We run the Stage 1b model (AUC=0.976) on all of Rwanda, aggregate predictions by GADM district, and correlate with the Rwanda Establishment Census (2020 + 2023).
We also present the error analysis requested by Sebastian (2026-03-06): where does the model make mistakes, and what spectral features distinguish errors from correct predictions?
For model training details, see Stage 1 and Stage 1b.

The pixel-level precision (0.195) is much lower than the patch-level training metric (0.951). This is expected — the model was trained on patches centered on buildings (positive sampling), and Open Buildings labels mark exact footprints while the model detects broader built-up areas.


4.2 False Positive Gallery



Top 16 worst FP patches. Green = ground truth buildings. Orange = false positives.


Key finding: Most “false positives” are actually roads, bare soil, parking areas, and urban fabric surrounding labeled buildings. These are genuinely built-up areas that Open Buildings (which only labels rooftops) doesn’t capture. This is a label limitation, not a model failure.


4.3 False Negative Gallery



Top 16 worst FN patches. Green = ground truth buildings. Blue = false negatives.


False negatives tend to be small, isolated buildings in vegetated areas where the 128×128 patch context is dominated by vegetation.


4.4 Spectral Profiles: What Distinguishes Errors?



Left: all 12 bands. Right: the 3 model input bands (NIR, Blue, SWIR2).


Critical finding: In the 3 model bands (right panel), FP and TP have nearly identical spectral signatures. The model literally cannot tell them apart with these bands alone. However, in the full 12-band plot (left panel), NDVI (band 11) clearly separates the categories — FPs have higher NDVI than TPs.


4.5 Confusion by NDVI and Brightness



Error rates binned by NDVI (left) and red brightness (right).


The NDVI plot tells the clearest story:

Low NDVI (bare/urban): High recall (~90%) but high FP rate (~50%) — the model detects almost everything non-vegetated
High NDVI (vegetation): Low FP rate but high miss rate — the model correctly ignores vegetation but misses buildings hidden under canopy

This is exactly what an NDVI input band would fix.

title: Stage 2: District Validation + Error Analysis
text:
5 Diagnosis: Why r = 0.38
The correlation is positive and statistically significant (p < 0.04), but below our target of r > 0.7. Three factors explain the gap:

Building count ≠ economic activity. Our model detects physical structures. A rural district with many scattered houses has lots of building pixels but few businesses. Kigali has dense commercial activity packed into fewer (but larger) buildings. Building count is a proxy for settlement extent, not economic intensity.
Label broadening. At threshold 0.90, the model detects built-up areas (roads, bare soil, construction) beyond just rooftops. This inflates pixel counts in arid/cleared districts relative to forested ones, decoupling from establishment counts.
No area normalization. Raw building pixel count scales with district area. Eastern districts (Nyagatare, Bugesera) are large and rank high on building pixels despite moderate establishment density.

title: Stage 2: District Validation + Error Analysis
text:
6 What We Learned

6.1 1. Binary Detection Has a Correlation Ceiling
Building count correlates with economic activity (r = 0.38) but has a natural ceiling around r ≈ 0.5. The reason is fundamental: binary detection measures settlement extent (how much land is covered by buildings and their surroundings), not economic intensity (how much activity happens per unit of settled area). Two districts with identical building coverage can have very different economic output — Kigali’s commercial districts vs. rural areas with dispersed homesteads.
Breaking through r > 0.7 requires either a regression head predicting intensity (establishments per pixel, trained on GPS-coded census data) or multi-source fusion (nightlights for intensity + daytime for extent).


6.2 2. The Model Detects Settlements, Not Buildings
The high FP rate against Open Buildings labels is misleading. The model detects the broader built environment — including roads, cleared land, and infrastructure around buildings. For economic mapping, this is arguably more useful than individual building footprints, because economic activity happens in the spaces between buildings (markets, roads, commercial strips), not only inside buildings.


6.3 3. Spectral Limitations Are Predictable
The spectral analysis confirms a prior that should guide feature engineering: if two classes are indistinguishable in the input feature space, no amount of training will separate them. FP and TP pixels overlap in NIR/Blue/SWIR2. The fix is not more training data or a larger model — it is more informative input bands. This is a feature engineering problem, not a model capacity problem.


6.4 4. NDVI Gradient Maps Directly to Error Gradient
The monotonic relationship between NDVI and FP rate means NDVI is not just “helpful” — it is the primary missing discriminant. Adding it as a 4th band is the single largest improvement available without changing labels or architecture.


6.5 5. Building Fraction Outperforms Raw Count
Building fraction (r = 0.541) already beats raw building pixel count (r = 0.382) without any retraining. This confirms that district area was a confound. Always normalize by area in future district-level analyses.

text:

7.1 No retraining needed:

Establishment density (per km²) to normalize both sides
Exclude Kigali to check rural-only correlation (Kigali dominates as leverage point)
Mean probability as predictor (r = 0.476, already better than raw count)



7.2 Model improvements:

Add NDVI as 4th input band — the error analysis shows this is the strongest FP/TP discriminator
buildingsALL on Quest (10-band unet_medium) — tests full spectral information
Water index (MNDWI) for lakeside districts



7.3 Strategic:

The ceiling for building detection → establishment count may be ~r ≈ 0.5. Reaching r > 0.7 likely requires the regression head (Stage 3) trained on actual GDP/sales data from Peru. Buildings alone proxy settlement size, not economic productivity.

title: Stage 2b: District Validation — 5-Experiment Comparison
text:
This report extends Stage 2 with district-level validation for all five models trained in Stage 1c. The question: which spectral configuration produces the most economically meaningful building density map?
Stage 1c showed that pixel-level AUC favors the simple NIR baseline (0.976) over all richer spectral inputs. But Stage 2 demonstrated that pixel AUC is a poor proxy for the downstream metric that matters — correlation with district-level economic activity. This report resolves the question by running full-country inference and district aggregation for each model.

3.2 Headline Result
NDVI achieves r = 0.750 (2023) — a 75% improvement over the NIR baseline’s r = 0.432. Adding a single NDVI band transforms a noisy economic proxy into a meaningful one.
This result confirms the Stage 2 error analysis prediction: NDVI separates false positives (bare soil, roads) from true buildings. title: Stage 2b: District Validation — 5-Experiment Comparison
text:
4 Detailed Analysis

4.1 1. NDVI: The Clear Winner
The NDVI model (4 bands: NIR, Blue, SWIR2, NDVI) achieves the best district-level correlation despite having lower pixel AUC than the baseline (0.941 vs 0.976). This is the central finding of Stage 1c/2b:

Pixel AUC does not predict district-level economic correlation. The metrics optimize for different things — AUC measures within-patch discrimination, while district r measures whether spatial aggregation of predictions tracks real economic activity across 30 diverse districts.

The NDVI model likely succeeds because it produces fewer false positives in rural/agricultural districts (high NDVI = vegetation = not a building), while maintaining true positives in urban areas (low NDVI = built-up). This reduces the noise that decoupled building pixel counts from establishment counts in the baseline model.


4.2 2. S2-all: Good but Redundant
The 10-band S2 model (r = 0.727, 2023) performs almost as well as NDVI (r = 0.750). The full S2 stack includes NDVI-correlated bands (Red, Red Edge) that implicitly encode vegetation information. However, the additional 6 bands beyond the NDVI model’s 4 don’t help — they add noise without new discriminative information for this task.
Recommendation: Use the 4-band NDVI model. It achieves 97% of the 10-band model’s district correlation with 60% fewer input bands, meaning faster inference, smaller data footprint, and simpler preprocessing.


4.3 3. AE-only: Catastrophic Failure
The Alpha Earth model produces negative correlations (r = -0.289 for 2020, r = -0.279 for 2023). This means districts with more predicted buildings have fewer actual establishments — the model’s spatial pattern is inverted relative to economic reality.
Root cause: Inspection of the AE-only predictions reveals that 11 out of 30 districts have zero building pixels. The model, operating on 64 learned embedding dimensions with a threshold of 0.05, fails to produce meaningful spatial variation. It detects “buildings” in a pattern that reflects Alpha Earth’s internal feature space (likely correlated with land cover type) rather than actual built-up areas. Districts with certain land cover profiles (e.g., dense agriculture) get high predictions while genuinely urban districts get low predictions.
This is not a training failure — the model converged (AUC = 0.921 on the validation set). The problem is that Alpha Earth embeddings encode land semantics at landscape scale, not building presence at structure scale. A 10m embedding that says “this area is agricultural” provides no information about whether a specific pixel contains a building.


4.4 4. S2+AE: AE Branch Poisons the Model
The dual-branch model produces negative correlations (r = -0.273 for 2020, r = -0.260 for 2023) — nearly identical to AE-only. Despite having a dedicated S2 encoder branch with the same spectral bands that achieve r = 0.73 in the S2-all model, the AE branch dominates the fused representation and imposes its inverted spatial pattern.
Multiple districts show 0% building pixels (Bugesera, Gisagara, Rwamagana, Kirehe, Kayonza, and notably Kicukiro — a Kigali district with 14,883 establishments). The dual-branch architecture does not protect the S2 signal from AE contamination at the fusion layer.
Conclusion: Alpha Earth embeddings are not just unhelpful — they actively degrade building detection when combined with S2 bands. The learned features encode spatial patterns that are anti-correlated with economic activity at district scale.

title: Stage 2b: District Validation — 5-Experiment Comparison
section: 5 The Key Insight: Pixel AUC vs. District Correlation
text:
5 The Key Insight: Pixel AUC vs. District Correlation
The results reveal a striking disconnect:
Pixel AUC ranking:     NIR (0.976) > NDVI (0.941) > S2-all (0.938) > S2+AE (0.931) > AE (0.921)
District r ranking:    NDVI (0.750) > S2-all (0.727) > NIR (0.432) > AE (-0.279)
The rankings are nearly inverted. The model with the best pixel-level discrimination produces the worst district-level economic signal (excluding AE’s catastrophic failure). Why?

Pixel AUC rewards confidence on the validation set. The NIR model is extremely confident (threshold 0.90) — it knows exactly which patches in the validation set are buildings. But this confidence doesn’t account for false positive patterns that vary systematically across districts.
District r rewards spatial consistency. What matters at district level is not whether each pixel is correct, but whether the aggregate building count per district is proportional to actual economic activity. The NDVI model makes more pixel-level errors (lower AUC) but makes them uniformly across districts, so the spatial pattern of aggregated predictions still tracks the census.
The NIR model’s false positives are geographically biased. Without NDVI, the model cannot distinguish bare soil from built-up areas. Arid eastern districts (Nyagatare, Bugesera) get inflated building counts from bare soil false positives, while forested western districts get suppressed counts. This geographic bias decorrelates predictions from the census.

This has a direct methodological implication: always evaluate satellite-derived economic proxies at the unit of economic analysis (district), not at pixel level. Pixel metrics are necessary for debugging but insufficient for validation.

title: Stage 2b: District Validation — 5-Experiment Comparison
text:
6 Recommendations

6.1 Immediate

Adopt the NDVI model as the production model for Rwanda. It achieves r = 0.75 at district level with only 4 input bands and the simplest preprocessing pipeline.
Retire the AE-only approach. Alpha Earth embeddings do not encode building presence at 10m resolution. They may be useful for land cover classification but not for structure-level detection.
Drop S2+AE. The dual-branch model (r = -0.27) performs as badly as AE-only — the AE branch overwhelms the S2 branch at fusion.



6.2 For Stage 3 (GDP Regression)
The r = 0.75 result means building density from the NDVI model explains ~56% of the variance in district-level establishment counts. This is a strong enough economic signal to serve as a feature in a GDP regression model. When Peru fine-GDP data becomes available, the NDVI building density map can be used as:

An input feature to a regression model predicting GDP per pixel
A stratification layer for sampling (dense vs. sparse built-up areas)
A spatial prior for the regression head (areas with no buildings should have near-zero GDP)



6.3 For Global Application
The 4-band NDVI model requires only NIR, Blue, SWIR2, and NDVI — all derivable from Sentinel-2, which is globally available and free. No dependency on Alpha Earth or other commercial/restricted datasets. This makes the NDVI model the most scalable option for the eventual global GDP map (Stage 4).

title: Dark Shipping Pipeline
text:
Fernández-Villaverde et al. (2025) estimate that 43% of global crude oil is dark shipped — vessels deliberately disable AIS transponders to avoid detection. Their analysis uses AIS gap episodes but cannot directly verify whether a vessel was actually present at a given location during a gap. Sentinel-1 SAR imagery can see through clouds and at night, making it the ideal sensor for independent validation.
Goal: Use Sentinel-1 SAR to validate AIS gap episodes near West Africa (Gulf of Guinea), building on the same U-Net pipeline used for Project A (building detection).

title: Dark Shipping Pipeline
text:
2 What Was Built
Four pipeline components were implemented and tested on 2026-03-05:

2.1 1. SAR Download (src/download/03_download_s1_dark_shipping.py)
Downloads Sentinel-1 GRD imagery from Google Earth Engine:

Polarization: VV only (optimal for ship detection — metallic hulls produce strong backscatter)
Orbit: Descending only (consistent viewing geometry across scenes)
AOI: Gulf of Guinea coastal zone (10°W–15°E, 0°N–8°N)
Compositing: Monthly mean composites (preserves ship signatures; median would smooth them out)
Pilot: Jan 2023 single-month export submitted to Google Drive



2.2 2. Ocean Grid (src/preprocessing/04_make_grid_west_africa.py)
Generates 0.5° grid cells over the Gulf of Guinea, land-masked via Natural Earth boundaries:



Metric
Value




Grid resolution
0.5° (~55.5 km at equator)


Total ocean cells
417


Pure ocean (>99%)
368


Coastal (mixed)
49


Min ocean fraction
10%



Output: tasks/dark-shipping-west-africa/labels/gulf_guinea_grid_halfdeg.shp


2.3 3. SAR Preprocessing (src/preprocessing/05_preproc_sar.py)
Standalone adaptation of the mlgis-ponds preprocessor for single-band SAR:

P2/P98 percentile normalization → rescaled to [-1, 1]
Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFF (COG) output
Two-stage workflow: --prerun for stats, then main tiling
Awaiting S1 pilot data from GEE to run



2.4 4. CFAR Ship Detection PoC (src/data/02_sar_ship_poc.py)
Proof-of-concept ship detection over Lagos Apapa Port using a CFAR-inspired algorithm:
For each pixel:
  threshold = local_mean + 3.5 × local_std
  ship = (VV > threshold) AND (VV > -10 dB absolute minimum)
  land_mask = exclude if local_mean > -8 dB
Parameters: guard window = 5 px, background window = 20 px, k = 3.5σ.

title: Dark Shipping Pipeline
text:
6 External Blockers
Three datasets are needed before U-Net training can begin:

AIS data. Register for free academic access at Global Fishing Watch. Needed to identify dark episodes (AIS blackouts + position gaps).
Ship training labels. Pre-labeled SAR ship datasets:

SUMO — European ship detections from Sentinel-1
Airbus Ship Detection Challenge — Kaggle dataset with SAR + optical

Dark score dataset. Email Fernández-Villaverde et al. (2025, NBER w33486) requesting vessel-level dark scores or collaboration.

title: Dark Shipping Pipeline
text:
7 Key Design Decisions







Decision
Rationale




Mean (not median) composites
Preserves ship signatures in short time windows


Descending orbit only
Consistent viewing angle across all scenes


Single VV band
Optimal for metallic hull detection; HV/VH less useful


Land masking via Natural Earth
Free, stable source; avoids hydrology complexity


CFAR threshold (adaptive)
Better than fixed dB in heterogeneous coastal zones


Standalone preprocessing
No vendor imports needed for 1-band; simpler dependencies
